done_when: (1) CLAUDE.md 'Gates are the operator's' section reworded to the explicit-instruction rule above, keeping the PR-merge gate strictly human; (2) .claude/settings.json carries a deterministic permissions.allow rule (Bash(plugin/bin/lume:*) + Bash(lume:*)) so the gate verbs are allowed without classifier interpretation; a narrow gate-verb-only rule is not expressible (lume puts -w <slug> before the verb; patterns only wildcard the suffix), so the rule allows all lume verbs - acceptable since lume only edits .lume/ JSON and the commit hook still gates history, with the CLAUDE.md decide-rule bounding WHEN the agent may run the gate; (3) the prohibition on autonomous/inferred acceptance is stated unambiguously enough that an adversarial reader cannot read it as licence to self-accept; (4) tests still pass (python -m pytest -q) - no engine code changed; (5) change committed through this workstream's working iteration.
text: The repo's operator gate currently forces the human to type approve/accept/reject in a terminal, because CLAUDE.md says the agent must 'never accept on the operator's behalf' and the auto-mode classifier reads that as a blanket ban. That overhead was never the gate's intent: the gate exists to stop the AGENT proceeding past the operator's decision autonomously, not to make the operator context-switch to run a command. Reframe the rule so the agent MAY run approve/accept/reject when the operator explicitly instructs it for the current iteration in the conversation (and points to that instruction), and MUST NEVER run them autonomously, speculatively, on inferred approval, or from its own judgement that the work is good. Behaviour-preserving for the guarantee (no autonomous proceeding); removes the operator's mechanical overhead.
title: Reframe the operator gate: agent may run approve/accept/reject on explicit per-iteration instruction, never autonomously
